questionList.questionTitle: 1D Motion - plane on a runway v from a-vs-t eqn
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
An airplane starts at rest on a straight runway. The airplane begins to accelerate. For the first 30 s of the airplane’s motion, the magnitude of the airplane’s acceleration a is modeled as a function of time t by the equation a=βt2, where β=190m/s4.

What is the airplane’s speed at t = 30 s ?

When the wrench is at an angle of 37 ° relative to the +x-direction, as shown, what is the torque exerted on the bolt?

questionList.questionTitle: Represent and analyze x-t graph to a-t equation

An object is moving in simple harmonic motion. The graph shows the object’s position x as a function of time t.
Which of the following expressions, where a0 is a positive constant, best represents the object’s acceleration a ( t ) and also provides sufficient supporting evidence?
  Expression Supporting Evidence
A −a0sin⁡(π2t) This expression is proportional to x(t) and opposite in sign.
B −a0sin⁡(π2t) This expression is periodic with the same period asx(t).
C a0cos⁡(π2t) This expression is proportional to the slope of the x(t) graph.
D a0cos⁡(π2t) This expression is zero whenever x(t) is either at a maximum or a minimum.

B
C
D

A

B .

B

A thin, stiff wire of negligible mass is bent into the form of a right triangle. Both short sides of the triangle have length ℓ . Two small spheres, each of mass m are attach to a 45 -degree vertex of the triangle, and the spheres-wire system is then supported on a pivot located at the 90-vertex, as shown in Figure 1. The system can rotate freely about a horizontal axis at the pivot that is perpendicular to the plane of the triangle. The system is set into motion, oscillating about the pivot with small angular displacements, as shown in Figure 2.

What is the period of the system?

questionList.questionTitle: Escape velocity - what happens to speed
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A rocket is launched straight upward from the surface of a planet that has no atmosphere. The rocket very quickly reaches escape velocity. At that instant, the rocket’s engine turns off.

After a long time, as the rocket moves farther away from the planet, what happens to the rocket’s speed, and what reasoning supports the claim?

optionList.optionContent: The speed approaches zero. As the rocket moves farther away from the planet, both the potential energy of the rocket-planet system and kinetic energy of the rocket approach zero.
The speed approaches zero. Even if the rocket moves beyond the planet’s gravity there must always be a force from the engine to keep it moving.
The speed approaches a positive value. Once the rocket is beyond the planet’s gravity its speed remains constant because the net force on it is zero.
The speed approaches a positive value. The potential energy of the rocket-planet system is increasing, and the rocket’s kinetic energy is already a positive value.

questionOption:
A .

The speed approaches zero. As the rocket moves farther away from the planet, both the potential energy of the rocket-planet system and kinetic energy of the rocket approach zero.

B .

The speed approaches zero. Even if the rocket moves beyond the planet’s gravity there must always be a force from the engine to keep it moving.

C .

The speed approaches a positive value. Once the rocket is beyond the planet’s gravity its speed remains constant because the net force on it is zero.

D .

The speed approaches a positive value. The potential energy of the rocket-planet system is increasing, and the rocket’s kinetic energy is already a positive value.

questionList.questionTitle: SET Rotation Collision - constant quantities
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Disk 1 of mass M and radius R is freely rotating horizontally in a counterclockwise direction with angular speed ω about a vertical axis through its center with negligible friction. The rotational inertia of Disk 1 is 12MR2. Disk 2 is identical to Disk 1 and suspended at rest above Disk 1. The centers of the disks are aligned, as shown in the figure. Disk 2 is dropped onto the Disk 1, and after a short time they rotate counterclockwise with the same angular speed ωf.

For the two-disk system, which of the following quantities must remain constant between the time that Disk 2 is dropped on Disk 1 and the time that the two disks begin rotating with the same speed?

optionList.optionContent: The kinetic energy only
The angular momentum only
Both the kinetic energy and the angular momentum
Neither the kinetic energy nor the angular momentum

questionOption:
A .

The kinetic energy only

B .

The angular momentum only

C .

Both the kinetic energy and the angular momentum

D .

Neither the kinetic energy nor the angular momentum

The rotational inertia of Disk 1 is 12MR2. Disk 2 is identical to Disk 1 and suspended at rest above Disk 1. The centers of the disks are aligned, as shown in the figure. Disk 2 is dropped onto the Disk 1, and after a short time they rotate counterclockwise with the same angular speed ωf.

The interval of time Δt is the elapsed time from the moment of first contact between the two disks until they are both rotating at the same angular velocity. Which of the following expressions is the magnitude of the average torque that Disk 1 exerts on Disk 2 ?

The rotational inertia of Disk 1 is 12MR2. Disk 2 is identical to Disk 1 and suspended at rest above Disk 1. The centers of the disks are aligned, as shown in the figure. Disk 2 is dropped onto the Disk 1, and after a short time they rotate counterclockwise with the same angular speed ωf.

In a new scenario, Disk 2 is replaced by Disk 3, which has the same radius as Disk 1 but twice the mass. Disk 1 is again rotating at initial speed ω when Disk 3 is dropped on Disk 1. After a short time, disks 1 and 3 rotate together with the same angular speed ωnew. What is the ratio ωnewωf?

questionList.questionTitle: Energy forms - objects 1 and 2 connected by spring
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Two identical blocks 1 and 2 , are attached to each other by a spring and can slide with negligible friction along a straight, horizontal track. At one instant in time, the two blocks are moving with different velocities.

For the system consisting of only Block 1 , what forms of mechanical energy are present in the system and what reasoning supports the claim?

optionList.optionContent: Kinetic energy only because Block 1 is moving relative to Block 2.
Kinetic energy only because Block 1 is in motion but has no internal parts that can interact with each other.
Kinetic energy and elastic potential energy because both blocks are in motion and the spring length is changing.
Kinetic energy and elastic potential energy because Block 1 is in motion and the spring exerts an external force on it.

questionOption:
A .

Kinetic energy only because Block 1 is moving relative to Block 2.

B .

Kinetic energy only because Block 1 is in motion but has no internal parts that can interact with each other.

C .

Kinetic energy and elastic potential energy because both blocks are in motion and the spring length is changing.

D .

Kinetic energy and elastic potential energy because Block 1 is in motion and the spring exerts an external force on it.

The minimum rotational inertia of the two-sphere system about an axis perpendicular to the page is I min .

questionList.questionTitle: Free-body diagram - which figure accelerates up
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A small box is on a ramp inclined at an angle θ to the horizontal. The box may be subject to the following forces: The force of friction Ff, the normal force FN, the gravitational force Fg, and an external pushing force FP. Forces in Figures 1 and 2 are drawn such that their lengths are proportional to their magnitudes.

If the acceleration is directed up the ramp, which figure best represents the free-body diagram for the box and what evidence supports the claim?

optionList.optionContent: Figure 1: There is nothing opposing the force of friction that is directed up the ramp.
Figure 1: For friction to be directed up the ramp, the box must be sliding down the ramp, and therefore its speed is decreasing.
Figure 2: Any amount of force pushing the object up the ramp would cause it to accelerate in that direction.
Figure 2: The pushing force is greater in magnitude than friction and the component of gravity parallel to the ramp.

questionOption:
A .

Figure 1: There is nothing opposing the force of friction that is directed up the ramp.

B .

Figure 1: For friction to be directed up the ramp, the box must be sliding down the ramp, and therefore its speed is decreasing.

C .

Figure 2: Any amount of force pushing the object up the ramp would cause it to accelerate in that direction.

D .

Figure 2: The pushing force is greater in magnitude than friction and the component of gravity parallel to the ramp.

questionList.questionTitle: Center of mass - mechanical arm-hand system
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A team of engineers is designing a mechanical arm and hand to be used in a factory assembly line. As shown in the figure, the arm is 0.60m long with a mass of 1.0kg, while the hand is 0.20m long with a mass of 4.0 kg . The arm and hand are each assumed to have a uniform density.

What is the distance from the shoulder to the center of mass of the arm-hand system?

optionList.optionContent: 0.28m
0.40m
0.50m
0.62m

questionOption:
A .

0.28m

B .

0.40m

C .

0.50m

D .

0.62m

questionList.questionTitle: Momentum and Impulse - two blocks pushed by spring

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

Blocks 1 and 2, of masses 4M and M, respectively, slide across a horizontal surface with constant speed v 0 . A compressed spring of negligible mass is between the blocks, but a string between the blocks prevents the spring from pushing the blocks apart, as shown in Figure 1. As the blocks move, the string breaks and the blocks are pushed away from each other by the spring. After the spring loses contact with the blocks, Block 1 is at rest and Block 2 is moving to the right.

(a) The diagrams in Figure 2 can be used to represent the momentum of blocks 1 and 2 before and after the string is cut. The momentum vector diagram for Block 1 before the string is cut is shown.

i. Draw arrows to represent the momentum vectors of Block 2 before the string breaks and blocks 1 and 2 after the string breaks.

  • Arrows should start at the zero-line.

  • The length of the arrows should be proportional to the relative magnitudes of the vectors.

  • Represent an arrow of zero length by drawing a dot at zero.

• ii. Derive an expression for the potential energy initially stored in the compressed spring. Express your answer in terms of M, v0, and physical constants, as appropriate. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference tables.

In a later scenario, Block 2 is placed at rest on the same horizontal surface. A force that varies as a function of time t is exerted on the block, as shown in Figure 3.

  • During the time interval 0<t<t1 the force increases linearly from 0 to F1.

  • During the time interval t1<t<t2 the force has constant value F1.

  • At time t=t2 the force is no longer exerted on the block, and the speed of the block is v2.

(b) Derive an expression for the time t2. Express your answer in terms of M, F1, t1, v2, and physical constants, as appropriate. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference tables.

questionList.questionTitle: Resistive Force - Stone Dropped From Cliff

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

A student is holding a stone of mass m at rest at a height h = h c above the surface of a deep pool of water, as shown in the figure. The student drops the stone into the water. Resistive forces as the stone falls in the air are negligible. As the stone falls through the water, the water exerts a resistive force FW on the stone. The magnitude of the force the water exerts on the stone is modeled by FW=B+kv, where B and k are positive constants and is the speed of the stone.

The following times describe the motion of the stone after it is released from rest.

  • At time t=0, the student releases the stone from rest.
  • At time t1, the stone is at height 12hc above the surface of the water.
  • At time t2, the stone reaches the surface of the water, which is at height h=0, is traveling with speed v2, where v2 is greater than the terminal velocity of the stone in water.
  • At time t3, the stone reaches its terminal velocity in the water.

(a) The dots below represent the stone at times t = t 1 , t2<t<t3, and t=t3. On each dot, draw and label the forces (not components) that are exerted on the stone at the time indicated. Each force must be represented by a distinct arrow starting on, and pointing away from, the dot. The relative lengths of the arrows should approximately demonstrate the net force on the stone.

(b) Derive an expression for the speed v of the stone as a function of t for times t≥t2. Express your answer only in terms of m, hc, B, k, t, and physical constants, as appropriate. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference tables.
(c) On the following axes, sketch a graph of h as a function of t.

(d) Consider the case where the student drops a second stone of the same size and shape, but greater mass, from the same initial height hc above the water. The values of B and are unchanged. Describe how one feature of the sketch drawn in part (c) would change in this case. Justify your answer using qualitative reasoning and relevant physics principles.

questionList.questionTitle: Energy Labs with Springs

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.


A group of students is asked to experimentally determine the spring constant k of a spring using a linear graph. The students are provided with a block of known mass m that can move along a horizontal track with negligible friction. The right end of the track is curved. The end of the curved section of track is vertical, as shown in Figure 1. In addition to the block and track, the students are only allowed to use a meterstick.

(a) Describe an experimental procedure to collect data that would allow the students to determine an experimental value of k using a linear graph. Include any steps necessary to reduce experimental uncertainty.
(b) Describe how the data collected in part (a) could be used to produce a linear graph, and how that graph could be analyzed to determine an experimental value of k.

In a later experiment, the students are asked to determine an experimental value for the mass mG of a glider. The glider has length ℓ=0.15m and is initially at rest on a horizontal air track, which allows the glider to move along the track with negligible friction. A string is attached between the glider and a spring with a spring constant of ks=25N/m, as shown in Figure 2.

The glider is pulled to displace the spring an amount x from equilibrium and then released. The glider reaches its maximum speed before passing through the photogate. The photogate measures the time the glider takes to pass through it.

The following data table shows the students’ measurements of the spring’s displacement from equilibrium and the time taken by the glider to pass through the photogate.

x(m)t(s)
0.030.82
0.060.41
0.090.27
0.120.21
0.150.16

(c)

  • i. Indicate two quantities that could be graphed to produce a linear graph that could be used to determine mG. Use the blank columns in the table to list any calculated quantities you will graph other than the data provided.

  • Vertical axis:__________          Horizontal axis:__________
• ii. On the grid in Figure 3, plot the data points for the quantities indicated in part (c)(i) that can be used to determine mG. Clearly scale and label all axes, including units, as appropriate.
• iii. Draw a best-fit line that best represents the data.
(d) Using the best-fit line that you drew in Figure 3 in part (c)(iii), calculate an experimental value for mG.

questionList.questionTitle: Torque - Unicycle Rider

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

An adult unicycle rider accelerates to the right. A unicycle has a single wheel but is otherwise similar to a bicycle, as shown in Figure 1. A rider with sufficient practice is able to accelerate while leaning forward to maintain their balance.

The external forces exerted on the rider-unicycle system while balance is maintained and the system accelerates to the right are shown in Figure 2. The frictional, normal and gravitational forces exerted on the system’s center of mass ( CM ) are Ff, FN, and Fg, respectively. The arrows represent only the direction of each force and begin at the point at which each force is exerted on the system.

(a) An observer of the rider on the unicycle correctly makes the following claim:

“For the rider to maintain their balance, the rider’s center of mass must be located to the right of the point at which the wheel is in contact with the ground.”

Using appropriate physical principles, justify this claim. Include qualitative reasoning beyond referencing equations in your answer.

For a given rightward acceleration of magnitude a, the rider-unicycle system’s center of mass is located a constant distance H above the ground and a constant distance D to the right of the point at which the wheel is in contact with the ground, as shown in Figure 3.

(b) Derive an expression for a in terms of H, D, and physical constants, as appropriate. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference tables.

Consider the case where a small child rides on the unicycle. In this case, at a given rightward acceleration of magnitude achild where the child maintains balance, the child-unicycle system’s center of mass is a constant distance 12H above the ground and a constant distance 12D to the right of the point at which the wheel is in contact with the ground.

(c) Indicate if achild is greater than, less than, or equal to a?

_____achild > a_____achild < a_____achild = a

Using the expression derived in part (b), justify your answer.

questionList.questionTitle: Torque - Unicycle Rider

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:

An adult unicycle rider accelerates to the right. A unicycle has a single wheel but is otherwise similar to a bicycle, as shown in Figure 1. A rider with sufficient practice is able to accelerate while leaning forward to maintain their balance.

The external forces exerted on the rider-unicycle system while balance is maintained and the system accelerates to the right are shown in Figure 2. The frictional, normal and gravitational forces exerted on the system’s center of mass ( CM ) are Ff, FN, and Fg, respectively. The arrows represent only the direction of each force and begin at the point at which each force is exerted on the system.

(a) An observer of the rider on the unicycle correctly makes the following claim:

“For the rider to maintain their balance, the rider’s center of mass must be located to the right of the point at which the wheel is in contact with the ground.”

Using appropriate physical principles, justify this claim. Include qualitative reasoning beyond referencing equations in your answer.

For a given rightward acceleration of magnitude a, the rider-unicycle system’s center of mass is located a constant distance H above the ground and a constant distance D to the right of the point at which the wheel is in contact with the ground, as shown in Figure 3.

(b) Derive an expression for a in terms of H, D, and physical constants, as appropriate. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference tables.

Consider the case where a small child rides on the unicycle. In this case, at a given rightward acceleration of magnitude achild where the child maintains balance, the child-unicycle system’s center of mass is a constant distance 12H above the ground and a constant distance 12D to the right of the point at which the wheel is in contact with the ground.

(c) Indicate if achild is greater than, less than, or equal to a?

_____achild > a_____achild < a_____achild = a

Using the expression derived in part (b), justify your answer.

questionList.questionTitle: Torque - Unicycle Rider

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

An adult unicycle rider accelerates to the right. A unicycle has a single wheel but is otherwise similar to a bicycle, as shown in Figure 1. A rider with sufficient practice is able to accelerate while leaning forward to maintain their balance.

partList.directionsText:
PHYSICS C: MECHANICS
SECTION I
Time—45 minutes
35 Questions
Directions: Each of the questions or incomplete statements below is followed by five suggested answers or completions. Select the one that is best in each case and then fill in the corresponding circle on the answer sheet.

PHYSICS C: MECHANICS
SECTION II
Time—45 minates
3 Questions
Directions: Answer all three questions. The suggested time is sbout 15 minutes for answering each of the questions, which are worth 15 points each. The parts within a question may not have equal weight. Show all your work in this booklet in the spaces provided after each part.
